title: Selfish Reasons to Have More Kids

chapters.title: The hidden joy of parenting
chapters.text:
When weighing the pros and cons of another child, most of us only see a small slice of the picture. We tend to focus on the costs that hit in the short run, since the early years of raising a child are a handful. After months of pregnancy and the strain of delivery, the mother and her partner still have hard months ahead.

So when parents ask whether to have another child, the cons usually win. One factor that people rarely consider, though, is that infancy is a passing stage and children last a lifetime. The benefits of having children grow with time.

As children get older, they become more fun and easier to be around. Look a little further into the future, and you will see that once your kids hit the teen years, they will barely have time for you. Once they are grown and on their own, you will be the one hoping for more phone calls and more visits. By then, even three children may not feel like enough.

As your children grow, their wins become your wins, and they come without extra work.

The right number of children for you can shift through the seasons of life. When you are a parent with toddlers tearing through the house, two can feel like a crowd. You may swear you will never have another child. If you push past that feeling and have two more, you will be the one to enjoy the rewards later on. When you are 30, four children can sound like too many. By the time you turn 60, each of your four children, and the grandchildren they bring, will likely be a deep source of joy.

So when you are weighing another child, do not make a fast call. It would be foolish to book a vasectomy just because your one-year-old is keeping you up at night. Instead of letting a rough patch shape a major life choice, think about the long arc. Weigh the short-term cons against the long-term pros, and look for a middle ground.

chapters.title: Streamline activities for easier parenting
chapters.text:
Back in the 60s and 70s, parents did not put themselves under the same pressure we do today. Studies from sociologists show that modern parents spend huge amounts of time taking care of their children. Fathers, for example, now spend more than seven hours a week on child care, which is over double the time dads spent per week in the 1970s. Mothers, in turn, have taken on far more on top of their old load.

So why has parenting become harder. Is it because kids are built differently these days. According to Bryan Caplan, children have not changed, but we have. Our modern style of parenting has turned children into a heavy load. We push ourselves to meet demands we could have simply said no to. We sign our kids up for karate, piano, French lessons, and more.

As a parent, you have to see that your whole life does not need to revolve around your kids. You can carve out free time with small, smart tweaks to your schedule.

To free up time for yourself, look at the chores and activities that give the least value and bring the most stress, and find ways to scale them back.

The simplest path is to cut the activities that you, and most importantly, your kids, do not enjoy. Let your children drop any class you pushed them into that has become a chore. Doing this will make your children happier and your life easier, since you will not have to keep driving your child to a piano lesson that makes them miserable.

chapters.title: Genes do most of the heavy lifting
chapters.text:
A lot of moms and dads stretch themselves thin because they assume the daily grind of child-rearing is what shapes the adult their kid becomes. They feel that stepping back for a moment is the same as risking their kid's future. Decades of careful work in behavioral science have shown, however, that this belief is largely a comforting story we tell ourselves.

Pulling back on the daily grind will not gamble with your kid's future.

The deeper truth is that, no matter how much love and labor you pour into your children, the DNA they were born with tends to do most of the steering over time. We all wish for our kids to grow up healthy, bright, content, successful, and good. How a child actually turns out is driven far more by the genes they inherited than by the schedule the parents kept. Even with a totally different family setup or a different style of raising, your child will most likely walk a similar road.

Biology outweighs upbringing over the long stretch of life, as Bryan Caplan has noted.

Long-running studies, including the Colorado Adoption Project and the Swedish Adoption and Twin Study of Aging, have lined up adopted children next to both their adoptive and biological parents. The pattern that came out of the data is striking. Adopted children often look a lot like their adoptive families while they are young. Once they reach adulthood, the resemblance shifts back toward their biological relatives, and the traits they shared with the adoptive family tend to fade away. Studies of identical and fraternal twins land on the same conclusion, which is that genes carry more weight than the home they grow up in.

Hearing that your hard work has little to no effect on your kid's future may feel like a letdown, yet you do not have to lose faith that your children will be fine. The real gift of this research is the permission to ease up. You do not have to burn yourself out to keep pace with other parents. Raise your kids in the way that feels right to you, and they will land on their feet either way.

chapters.title: The world is the safest it has ever been for kids
chapters.text:
For most moms and dads, the loudest anxiety is whether their children are physically safe, and the press does not soften that worry. If you sit through a lot of evening news or you love a good true crime series, you probably believe the modern era is a scary place to raise a child.

What the press does not spell out is that the tragedies you watch on scripted crime shows are pure fiction, and the ones you see on cable news, while real, do not reflect the odds. The chances that a heartbreaking story you saw on screen will land on you or someone you love are vanishingly small. There is no good reason to make a major life call, like whether to start a family, on the basis of what flashed across the TV.

In spite of the headlines, the modern era is a remarkably calm place to raise a child.

Across recorded time, no era has been safer for the young. Even though the 1950s are often painted as the lost golden age of childhood, the data shows that families, and especially the youngest among them, live in far safer conditions now. In the 1950s, more than three out of every 100 babies did not see their first birthday. That kind of loss is rare today.

For most of history, illness was the leading killer of the young, and modern medicine has tamed many of those threats. Widespread immunization has nearly wiped out deaths from contagious disease.

In 1950, flu and pneumonia together were behind about 14 percent of infant deaths. By 2005, the chance that an infant would die from those illnesses had fallen by roughly thirtyfold.

If you already have children, or you hope to one day, take heart that you are raising them in a uniquely safe stretch of history, as Bryan Caplan reminds us.

In a planet of eight billion people, the press will always have a heartbreaking or terrifying story to splash on the screen. That should not be the reason you skip having children, and it should not be the reason you fear for a child you already have. Kids today are well protected, and this is the era that lets parents rest easy at night, knowing their children are out of harm's way.

A small data point for context. The 2015 National Vital Statistics Report from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention put the annual chance of death for children at 16.3 out of 100,000. That is a 99.9837 percent chance of staying alive through the year.

chapters.title: More kids, more good for the world
chapters.text:
When weighing whether to have children, we often wonder if a new life will make the world better or worse. Most people, sadly, lean toward worse. The classic argument is that population growth drains resources, and that drain ends in poverty.

The complaint is not wrong on the surface, but it is not the whole story. Yes, babies do nothing but eat and use resources. The truth is that children do not stay babies. They grow up, stop being a net drain, and add value to the world around them.

More babies will not hurt the world. We need more people to keep human progress going.

The world is more prosperous today than it was 100 years ago, and the main reason is the rise in population. Progress comes from great ideas, and great ideas come from smart, creative people. Beyond the people who come up with the ideas, you need a large group of buyers to turn creativity into paying work. There is little reason for creative minds to keep pushing if there are not billions of other people ready to value the products they spend years building.

So even if you have 10 or 20 children, the early stretch will wear you out, but the size of your family does not make the world poorer. Your child may grow into a creative mind with extraordinary ideas to offer. Even if your child is not a genius, they will likely grow into a productive member of society. The choice to have children adds to the world, and it does not add to poverty.

chapters.title: The family payoff shows up later
chapters.text:
The payoff of raising kids rarely shouts in the early years, since those years are loud, messy, and tiring. The deeper rewards are waiting on the other side, and they grow quietly as the years pass.

As children grow, they grow into their own people, and that growth becomes a quiet source of pride. The late-night feedings and the toddler tantrums fade, and what stays is the human you helped shape. Watching them become a kind, capable adult is one of the slow gifts of the work.

The bond with grown children can be one of the richest of your life. By the time your children are settled and starting families of their own, you get to enjoy a closeness that asks for less of your energy. You become a sounding board, a trusted advisor, and a steady presence in their days.

Grandchildren are a chapter of their own. They bring joy without the weight of daily care, and they let you enjoy the small moments of childhood again. Many parents say grandparenthood is when the full reward of having children finally lands.

The friendships you build through your children are another quiet payoff. Other parents become an extended circle, and the play dates and school events turn into lasting ties. Those friendships often outlast the years of active parenting and add to the texture of your life.

So the case for adding to your family is not only about the short stretch of diapers and lost sleep. It is about the long arc of family, the slow build of pride, and the deep ties that span decades. The investment is heavy at the start, but the returns grow across a lifetime.

chapters.title: Wrapping up the case for more children
chapters.text:
Today, a surprising number of would-be parents are losing out on something important. They are letting their dread of the hard parts of raising a child keep them from the chance to deepen their lives with another baby or two. Turning down the gift of a child who could have been a deep source of warmth is a sad missed opportunity.

Almost every grown-up can rattle off a long list of reasons not to have kids. What most of us have never been taught, though, are the reasons to bring new life into the world. Our joy and well-being as individuals are tied to that choice, and so is the survival of the progress our species has built up over centuries.

Against the conventional wisdom, bringing more children into the world makes it a better place. For hundreds of years, the global population has climbed, and the standard of living has climbed with it. That is no coincidence. Fresh ideas are the engine behind rising living standards, and the wellspring of fresh ideas is people. Inventive minds uncover breakthroughs, and the marketplace rewards their work with paying customers. The more people we have on the planet, the more breakthroughs we get to enjoy.

If you let the immediate cost of life with a toddler cloud your judgment when weighing another child, you are shortchanging yourself. The wins of having children usually arrive later in the journey. Do not let a few months of broken sleep and cranky days stand between you and your future as a content parent and grandparent.

Raising kids is stressful, no question, yet a lot of that stress is self-inflicted. As a parent, you can enjoy a calm and steady life without putting your kids or their future at risk.

A small practice to try. Be gentle with yourself. Pick the parenting style that fits who you are, and resist the urge to keep up with other parents. Ask yourself these three questions before doing anything for your child. Does this bring me joy. Does it bring my child joy. Will it pay off in the long run. If the answer is yes, do it with your full heart. If an activity fails any of those tests, drop it from the schedule without guilt.

If you read to the end, well done.
